fuzzy_prd:
We need a unified SMS dispatch library that works across multiple provider back-ends without callers having to know each provider's particular request format. The library should handle: (1) normalizing a recipient phone number — the caller provides a local number and optionally an international calling code (which might have a leading plus or leading zeros); the library should expose the raw number, the parsed country code, a plus-prefixed universal form, a double-zero-prefixed form, and a JSON representation; (2) reading and mutating a nested credential configuration using dot-notation keys, including descending into nested objects and numbered list entries; (3) modeling a message as either a simple string or a richer object with content, template ID, template variables, and a preferred list of delivery providers; (4) resolving a named delivery provider — short names should be matched case-insensitively and ignoring hyphens and underscores, with a configurable default — and failing fast with a normalized error when the name is unknown or no default has been set; (5) determining the effective request timeout for a provider, with a precedence hierarchy: per-instance override wins, then config-supplied value, then a built-in fallback; and (6) assembling the exact outbound HTTP request for each supported provider and normalizing the provider's reply into either an accepted outcome or a consistent error with the provider's own code and message. Follow the same signature-prefix convention that the message-validation layer uses for the first listed provider.

One small follow-up from the questions that came in: for the phone normalization bit, the IDD code is normalized by stripping all leading '+' and '0' characters. So '+68', '068', '68', and '0068' all parse to integer 68. If no IDD code is provided at all, then the universal and zero_prefixed forms should just be the bare local number rather than trying to add anything.

On timeout behavior, the built-in default timeout is 5.0 seconds. A 'timeout' key in the config map overrides the default, and an explicit per-instance set_timeout override still takes highest precedence over both. When we expose or compare the effective value, keep the output format to one decimal place, e.g. '5.0'.

Also, on provider lookup, the matching rule is case-insensitive and removes hyphens and underscores before comparison. So for a concrete example, 'error-log', 'ErrorLog', and 'error_log' should all resolve to the 'errorlog' provider.

trigger_keywords: when is the signature prepended to message text
signature bracket character for yunpian provider
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For the yunpian provider, a configured 'signature' is prepended to the message text only when the text does NOT already start with '【'. If the text already begins with '【', it is sent unchanged.

trigger_keywords: what is the default message type
content and template defaults for message
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Default message type is 'text'. When message is a bare string: content=that string, template=that string, data=[], gateways=[]. When message is an object, missing fields default to: content='', template='', data=[], gateways=[].

trigger_keywords: dot notation key accessing list index
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A null key in a get operation returns null. Dot-notation descends through nested maps and numerically-indexed list entries: 'numbers.0.id' reads index 0 of the 'numbers' list and then key 'id'. Output format: 'get <key> => <json-value>\n' (null keys shown as '(null)').

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the signature-prefix convention that the message-validation layer uses for the first listed provider
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The Yunpian gateway's request-building logic: it prepends the configured 'signature' string (formatted as '【signature】') to the message content only when the content does not already start with '【'.

oracle_data.fallback_response: Refer to the src/ directory for the PhoneNumber, Message, Config, and gateway classes. The rcb_tests/dispatcher/dispatch.php shows the complete action-to-class mapping.

feature1_phone_number.json.description: Normalize a subscriber phone number that may carry an international dialing (IDD) country code. The number is provided as the local number plus an optional IDD code; the code may be given as a bare integer, with a leading plus sign, or with leading zeros, and all three forms denote the same country code. The component exposes the raw local number, the parsed integer country code (empty when none was supplied), a '+'-prefixed universal form, a '00'-prefixed form, and a JSON serialization that equals the universal form. When no IDD code is present, the universal and zero-prefixed forms are identical to the bare local number.

feature2_config_access.json.description: Read from and mutate a nested configuration map using dot-notation keys. A `get` resolves a key against the map: a top-level key returns its value, a dotted key descends into nested maps and numerically-indexed lists, and a key that does not resolve (or a null key) yields null. An existence check reports whether a top-level key is present. An assignment updates the value of an already-present top-level key, and a removal deletes a present top-level key; after removal a subsequent read of that key returns null. Each operation emits one line describing its result, with scalar values rendered as JSON.

cases.expected_output:
isset foo => true
get foo => "bar"
get (null) => null
get bar.profile.id => 9999
get bar.profile.name => "overtrue"
get numbers.0.id => 1
get numbers.0.number => 1
get numbers.1.id => 2
get numbers.1.number => 2
set foo
get foo => "new-bar"
unset foo
get foo => null

feature3_message.json.description: Construct a message envelope. When the message is supplied as a bare string, that string becomes both the message content and the template identifier. When supplied as a structured object, the individual attributes (content, template, template data, and the list of preferred gateways) are stored as given. In all cases the message type defaults to text, content and template default to empty, template data defaults to an empty collection, and the preferred-gateway list defaults to empty. The component reports the resolved type, content, template, template data, and gateway list.

cases.expected_output:
type=text
content=文本
template=文本
data=[]
gateways=[]

feature4_1_gateway_resolve.json.description: Resolve a delivery gateway by its short name. A short name is mapped to a built-in gateway provider and, on success, the gateway's canonical lower-case name is reported. The resolver may also be configured with a default gateway up front; when no explicit name is requested, the default is resolved instead.

feature4_2_unknown_gateway.json.description: Attempt to resolve a gateway by a name that does not correspond to any known built-in provider. Resolution fails and a neutral error category is reported together with the offending requested name.

feature4_3_no_default_gateway.json.description: Request the default gateway when none has been configured. Resolution fails fast with a neutral error category indicating that no default gateway is available.

feature5_gateway_timeout.json.description: Determine the effective request timeout (in seconds) of a resolved gateway. When the configuration specifies no timeout, a built-in default of 5 seconds applies. A timeout supplied in the configuration overrides the default. An explicit per-instance timeout override, once set, takes precedence over both. The effective timeout is reported as a one-decimal value.

feature6_1_yunpian_request.json.description: Build the outbound HTTP request for a form-encoded SMS provider whose single-send endpoint takes an API key, the recipient's universal number, and the message text. A configured signature prefix is prepended to the text only when the text does not already begin with the signature-bracket character; text that already starts with that bracket is sent unchanged. The provider's JSON reply carries a numeric result code: a zero code means accepted, and any non-zero code is surfaced as a normalized gateway error carrying that code and the provider's message. The output reports the request method, endpoint URL, the sorted form parameters, and the outcome.

cases.expected_output:
method=POST
url=https://sms.yunpian.com/v2/sms/single_send.json
param.apikey=mock-api-key
param.mobile=18188888888
param.text=【测试】This is a 【test】 message.
outcome=sent

method=POST
url=https://sms.yunpian.com/v2/sms/single_send.json
param.apikey=mock-api-key
param.mobile=18188888888
param.text=【overtrue】This is a test message.
outcome=error
error_code=100
error_message=发送失败

feature6_2_twilio_request.json.description: Build the outbound HTTP request for an international form-encoded SMS provider. The account identifier is interpolated into the per-account messages endpoint path, and the request carries the recipient in '+'-prefixed universal form together with the configured sender and the message body. The output reports the request method, endpoint URL, the sorted form parameters, and the outcome.

cases.expected_output:
method=POST
url=https://api.twilio.com/2010-04-01/Accounts/mock-api-account-sid/Messages.json
param.Body=【twilio】This is a test message.
param.From=mock-from
param.To=+8618888888888
outcome=sent

feature6_3_juhe_request.json.description: Build the outbound HTTP request for a template-based SMS provider that uses an HTTP GET with query parameters. The query carries the recipient's local number, the template identifier, the template variables (URL-encoded as a '#name#'-keyed query string), a JSON response-format flag, and the API key. The provider's JSON reply carries a numeric error code: zero means accepted, and any non-zero code is surfaced as a normalized gateway error with that code and the provider's reason. The output reports the request method, endpoint URL, the sorted query parameters, and the outcome.

cases.expected_output:
method=GET
url=http://v.juhe.cn/sms/send
param.dtype=json
param.key=mock-key
param.mobile=18188888888
param.tpl_id=mock-tpl-id
param.tpl_value=%23code%23=1234
outcome=sent

method=GET
url=http://v.juhe.cn/sms/send
param.dtype=json
param.key=mock-key
param.mobile=18188888888
param.tpl_id=mock-tpl-id
param.tpl_value=%23code%23=1234
outcome=error
error_code=21000
error_message=操作失败

feature6_4_submail_request.json.description: Build the outbound HTTP request for a project-based SMS provider whose endpoint is selected by the recipient's region: recipients with no country code or the mainland country code route to the domestic send endpoint, while any other country code routes to the international send endpoint. The form parameters carry the application id, the signature credential, the project id (taken from the message data when present, otherwise from configuration), the recipient in '+'-prefixed universal form, and the template variables encoded as a JSON object. The provider's JSON reply carries a status: a success status means accepted, and any other status is surfaced as a normalized gateway error with the reply's code and message. The output reports the request method, endpoint URL, the sorted form parameters, and the outcome.

cases.expected_output:
method=POST
url=https://api.mysubmail.com/message/xsend.json
param.appid=mock-app-id
param.project=mock-project
param.signature=mock-app-key
param.to=18188888888
param.vars={"code":"123456","time":"15"}
outcome=sent

method=POST
url=https://api.mysubmail.com/internationalsms/xsend.json
param.appid=mock-app-id
param.project=mock-project
param.signature=mock-app-key
param.to=+118188888888
param.vars={"code":"123456","time":"15"}
outcome=sent
